title: Rank and Null Space
summary: Understand the rank of a matrix, null space, and the fundamental theorem of linear algebra.
content_html:
Rank of a Matrix

The rank of matrix A, denoted rank(A) or r(A), is the number of non-zero rows in its row echelon form (= number of pivot positions).

A = [1  2  3]   REF→  [1  2  3]   rank = 2
    [2  4  6]          [0  0  0]
    [0  1  2]          [0  1  2]

Wait, let me redo: after proper row-reduction, we find rank(A) = number of pivots.

Full Rank

  • An m×n matrix has full column rank if rank(A) = n (all columns are independent).
  • It has full row rank if rank(A) = m (all rows are independent).

Null Space (Kernel)

The null space of A is the set of all vectors x such that Ax = 0:

Null(A) = {x : Ax = 0}

The null space is always a subspace (contains zero vector, closed under addition/scalar multiplication).

Nullity

The nullity of A = number of free variables = n − rank(A).

Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra

For an m×n matrix A:

rank(A) + nullity(A) = n

This means: (number of pivot columns) + (number of free columns) = total columns.

Solvability Condition

The system Ax = b is consistent (has at least one solution) if and only if:

rank(A) = rank([A|b])

If rank(A) = rank([A|b]) = n: unique solution.
If rank(A) = rank([A|b]) < n: infinitely many solutions.
If rank(A) < rank([A|b]): no solution.

Example

A = [1  0  2]   After REF:  [1  0  2]   → rank = 2
    [0  1  3]               [0  1  3]     nullity = 3-2 = 1
    [0  0  0]               [0  0  0]

Null space: x = t[-2, -3, 1]ᵀ  for any scalar t
